required.title: [Primary Texts] At least three complete works of recognized literary quality from the 3.2.4-B reading list
[Reference] A style guide for citation — The Chicago Manual of Style
required.author: student edition) or *MLA Handbook*

required.title: [For Mentor] Access to a library — physical or digital

suggested.title: [Reference] How to Read a Book
suggested.author: Mortimer Adler and Charles Van Doren

competence_markers.description: The student can distinguish between primary, secondary, and tertiary sources and explain when each is appropriate
The student can find at least five relevant sources on a literary topic using a library catalog, databases, and citation chaining
The student takes organized research notes that clearly distinguish between direct quotation, paraphrase, and personal response, with source information recorded for every entry
The student can build a properly formatted bibliography in at least one standard citation style (Chicago or MLA)
The student can explain what plagiarism is, identify it in sample passages, and articulate why it matters beyond institutional punishment
The student can formulate a genuine research question about a literary text and pursue an answer through systematic research
The student has completed at least one research exercise with proper sourcing, notes, and citation
The student can evaluate a source for reliability, authority, and potential bias

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain the difference between a primary source, a secondary source, and a tertiary source.
narration_prompts.detail: Give an example of each for a book you are reading.

narration_prompts.prompt: Walk me through your note-taking system.
narration_prompts.detail: Show me a set of notes you took. Point to a direct quotation, a paraphrase, and one of your own ideas. How do you keep them separate?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is plagiarism? Why does it matter beyond just getting in trouble?
narration_prompts.detail: What does it do to the trust that makes intellectual work possible?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about your research question.
narration_prompts.detail: What are you trying to find out? Why does the question interest you? What sources have you found so far? What have you learned that you did not expect?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick a source you found and evaluate it.
narration_prompts.detail: Who wrote it? What are their qualifications? Is it reliable? How do you know? Would you use it in a paper? Why or why not?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is a bibliography, and why does it matter?
narration_prompts.detail: It is not just a list — what purpose does it serve for the reader of your paper?

cross_connections.description: This is the primary companion packet. While 3.2.4-B1 teaches research skills in the context of literary study, 3.2.6-B develops the same skills across all disciplines and goes deeper into source evaluation, the ethics of citation, and the full research paper. The two packets reinforce each other directly.
B1 teaches the student to find and organize information; B2 teaches the student to write it up with scholarly precision. They are two halves of the same capability.

The research skills learned here produce the evidence that argumentative writing in 3.2.2 requires. A student who can find and evaluate sources can write arguments grounded in reality rather than opinion.

Historical research uses the same skills: source-finding, note-taking, evaluation, citation. The student who learns research through literature can transfer the skills directly to historical scholarship.

Source evaluation — asking who created this, for what purpose, with what bias — is the same skill that propaganda analysis requires. The student who can evaluate a literary source can evaluate a news source.
Plagiarism avoidance is not a skill — it is an expression of character. The student who cites honestly because they understand why honesty matters has internalized the integrity principles of IC1.3.

content.why_this_matters:
You have been hearing and reading great stories for years. Now you are going to learn how to study them — not just as a reader who follows the plot, but as a researcher who can find information about texts, take careful notes, build a bibliography, and investigate what other readers and scholars have said about the books you read. These are the skills that turn a reader into a scholar.

Research is not the same as looking something up. Looking something up gives you one answer to one question. Research is a process: you start with a question you genuinely want to answer, you find multiple sources, you evaluate whether those sources are reliable, you take notes that you can actually use later, and you organize what you have found into something coherent. Along the way, you learn to give credit to the people whose ideas you are using — not because someone forces you to, but because intellectual honesty requires it.

These skills apply far beyond literature. Every serious field — history, science, law, medicine, philosophy — depends on the same research process. But literature is an excellent place to learn it, because literary research requires you to work with primary texts (the actual books), secondary sources (what scholars have written about those books), and your own thinking. You will learn to find sources, evaluate them, take notes without accidentally stealing someone's words, and build a bibliography that a reader can follow. These are foundational skills that you will use for the rest of your intellectual life.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Source-finding capability** — the student can locate books, articles, and reliable online resources relevant to a literary topic
2. **Note-taking discipline** — the student takes organized, usable notes that distinguish between direct quotation, paraphrase, and personal response
3. **Bibliography construction** — the student can build a properly formatted bibliography for any research project
4. **Source evaluation** — the student can assess whether a source is reliable, relevant, and authoritative
5. **Plagiarism understanding** — the student understands what plagiarism is, why it matters, and how to avoid it
6. **The research question** — the student can formulate a genuine question about a literary text and pursue an answer through research
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Research Is and Is Not

Research is not Googling a topic and reporting what you find. That is looking things up. Research is a disciplined process of inquiry:

1. **You start with a question** — not a topic, but a question. "Shakespeare" is a topic. "Why does Shakespeare make Puck the final speaker in *A Midsummer Night's Dream*?" is a question. The question drives everything else.

2. **You find sources** — books, articles, scholarly essays, reliable websites. You do not stop at the first source. You find multiple sources and see where they agree and where they disagree.

3. **You evaluate sources** — not everything written about a topic is equally reliable. A professor's published essay about Shakespeare is more authoritative than a random blog post. A peer-reviewed journal article is more reliable than a Wikipedia summary (though Wikipedia can be a useful starting point for finding real sources through its citations).

4. **You take notes** — careful, organized notes that record where every piece of information came from and whether you are writing down someone else's exact words, paraphrasing their idea, or recording your own thought.

5. **You synthesize** — you put together what you have found, compare sources, identify patterns, and develop your own interpretation.

6. **You cite** — you give credit to every source you used, in a format that lets a reader find those sources themselves.

This process is the same whether you are researching a Shakespeare play, the history of the Roman Empire, or the effectiveness of a new medical treatment. The skills are universal. You are learning them here through literature, but you will use them everywhere.

### Part 2: Finding Sources

**The library is your most important tool.** Physical libraries and their digital extensions (online catalogs, databases, interlibrary loan) provide access to resources that no internet search can match. A reference librarian is a trained expert in finding information — use them.

**Types of sources for literary research:**

- **Primary sources:** The literary texts themselves. If you are researching *The Odyssey*, the primary source is *The Odyssey*. Everything else is secondary. Primary sources are always the most important evidence.
- **Secondary sources:** What scholars have written about the primary text. Published literary criticism, scholarly essays, book-length studies. These are found in academic journals, university press books, and scholarly databases.
- **Tertiary sources:** Encyclopedias, textbook summaries, SparkNotes, CliffsNotes. These can help you understand the basics, but they are not evidence for a research paper. They are entry points, not destinations.
- **Contextual sources:** Historical, biographical, and cultural information that helps you understand the world in which the text was written. Understanding that *Animal Farm* was written during World War II as an allegory of the Russian Revolution is not literary analysis — it is context that makes literary analysis possible.

**How to find sources:**
- Start with your library's catalog and databases
- Use the bibliography of a good secondary source to find more sources (this is called "citation chaining" and it is one of the most effective research strategies)
- Ask a reference librarian for help — this is literally their job and they are good at it
- Use Google Scholar for academic articles (but verify access through your library)
- Be skeptical of any source that does not itself cite sources

### Part 3: Note-Taking for Research

Bad note-taking is the most common cause of accidental plagiarism and wasted research time. Good note-taking requires a system.

**The three types of research notes:**

1. **Direct quotation:** You copy the author's exact words and put them in quotation marks. You record the page number. You do this when the author's specific language matters — when you might want to quote them in your paper.

2. **Paraphrase:** You restate the author's idea in your own words. You still record the source and page number, because the idea is theirs even though the words are yours. A paraphrase should be genuinely in your own language — not the original sentence with a few words swapped.

3. **Personal response:** Your own thoughts, reactions, and questions in response to what you read. Mark these clearly so you never confuse your ideas with someone else's.

**For every note, record:**
- Author's name
- Title of the work
- Page number (or URL and access date for digital sources)
- Whether the note is a direct quote, a paraphrase, or your own thought

**Practical systems:**
- Note cards (one idea per card, source information at the top)
- A research notebook with a consistent format (left column for source info, right column for notes)
- Digital note-taking with clear tags for source, quote, paraphrase, and response

The system matters less than the consistency. Pick one and use it every time.

### Part 4: Building a Bibliography

A bibliography is a list of every source you consulted for a research project, formatted so that a reader can find each source themselves. It is not busywork. It is an act of intellectual honesty — a way of saying, "Here is where my ideas came from. Check them if you want."

**Basic bibliography format (Chicago style):**

For a book:
Last Name, First Name. *Title of Book*. Place of Publication: Publisher, Year.

For a journal article:
Last Name, First Name. "Title of Article." *Journal Name* Volume, no. Issue (Year): Page range.

For a website:
Last Name, First Name (if available). "Title of Page." Website Name. Last modified date or access date. URL.

You will learn the specific details of formatting from your style guide. The principle is more important than the format: every source gets credited, and the credit is specific enough for someone to find it.

### Part 5: Plagiarism — What It Is and Why It Matters

Plagiarism is presenting someone else's words or ideas as your own. It includes:

- **Copying someone's words without quotation marks and citation** — even a single sentence
- **Paraphrasing someone's idea without giving them credit** — even if you changed every word
- **Submitting someone else's work as your own** — including work written by a friend, a tutor, or an AI
- **Patchwriting** — cobbling together phrases from multiple sources with minor word changes, creating the illusion of original writing

Plagiarism is not a technical violation. It is a form of dishonesty. When you present someone's idea as your own, you are taking credit for their thinking. When you copy their words, you are stealing their craft. In the professional world, plagiarism ends careers. In the academic world, it results in expulsion. In this framework, the consequence is not a punishment — it is a serious conversation about integrity and a requirement to redo the work honestly.

**How to avoid plagiarism:**
- Take careful notes that distinguish between quotation, paraphrase, and your own ideas
- When in doubt, cite. Over-citation is a minor stylistic issue. Under-citation is a moral failure.
- Develop your own ideas. A paper that is nothing but other people's ideas stitched together, even with proper citation, is not a research paper — it is a book report. The research supports YOUR argument.
- If you use someone's exact words, put them in quotation marks and cite the source. Period.

### Part 6: The Research Question

The quality of your research depends on the quality of your question. A vague question produces vague research. A specific question focuses your work and gives you a standard for what counts as a relevant source.

**Weak research questions:**
- "What is *The Odyssey* about?" (This is a summary question, not a research question.)
- "Is Shakespeare good?" (Too vague to research.)
- "What do scholars say about *To Kill a Mockingbird*?" (This produces a survey, not an argument.)

**Strong research questions:**
- "Why does Odysseus lie so often, and what does that tell us about what the Greeks valued?"
- "How does Shakespeare use Puck to comment on the nature of theater itself?"
- "Why did Harper Lee choose to tell the story of Tom Robinson's trial through a child's perspective?"

A good research question is specific enough to answer, interesting enough to pursue, and complex enough that the answer is not obvious. You will refine your questions as you research — the question you start with is rarely the question you end with, because research changes your understanding.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Library Orientation [Early in Packet]
Visit a physical library (public or academic) with your mentor. Meet a reference librarian. Learn to use the catalog. Locate the literature section, the reference section, and any available databases. The student should leave this visit knowing how to find a book by author, title, or subject, and how to request a book through interlibrary loan.

### Study 2: Source-Finding Exercise [Early Packet]
Choose a literary text the student is currently reading. Together, find at least five sources about that text: the text itself (primary), at least two scholarly secondary sources (articles or book chapters), at least one contextual source (biography or historical background), and at least one tertiary source (encyclopedia entry or study guide). Discuss: which source is most reliable? Which would you use in a paper? Which would you not use, and why?

### Study 3: Note-Taking Practice [Mid-Packet]
The student reads one scholarly article about a literary text and takes research notes using the three-note system (quotation, paraphrase, personal response). The student produces at least 15 note cards or entries. The mentor reviews the notes for: proper quotation marking, clear distinction between paraphrase and quotation, source information recorded with every note, and evidence of the student's own thinking.

### Study 4: Bibliography Workshop [Mid-Packet]
The student builds a properly formatted bibliography of at least eight sources — the sources found in Studies 2 and 3, plus additional sources found independently. The bibliography uses correct Chicago or MLA format. The mentor checks format against the style guide and discusses any errors.

### Study 5: Plagiarism Case Study [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Discuss real examples of plagiarism and its consequences. Present the student with sample passages and ask them to identify: which ones are properly cited? Which ones are plagiarized? Which are in the gray area? Discuss why the gray areas exist and how to handle them. The goal is understanding, not fear.

### Study 6: The First Research Project [Late Packet]
The student formulates a genuine research question about a literary text, finds at least five sources, takes organized notes, builds a bibliography, and writes a research summary of 500-800 words that answers their question using evidence from their sources with proper citation. This is not a full research paper — it is a research exercise that practices every step of the process.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Source-Finding Capability
**Developing well:** The student can independently find multiple relevant sources using a library catalog, databases, and citation chaining. They move beyond the first page of Google results. They ask librarians for help when stuck.

**Developing slowly:** The student relies entirely on internet search and cannot navigate a library catalog or database. Sources are limited to whatever appears first in a search.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had a proper library orientation? Have you modeled the source-finding process? Does the student have access to the tools they need?

### 2. Note-Taking Discipline
**Developing well:** Notes are consistently organized, with clear distinction between quotation, paraphrase, and personal response. Source information is recorded with every entry. Notes are usable — the student can return to them weeks later and still understand what they recorded and where it came from.

**Developing slowly:** Notes are disorganized, missing source information, or fail to distinguish between the student's words and the source's words.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student been taught a specific system? Have you reviewed notes early enough to correct problems before bad habits solidify? Is the system too complex for the student's current ability? (Simplify if needed — the system should serve the work, not complicate it.)

### 3. Citation Accuracy
**Developing well:** Bibliographies are correctly formatted in the chosen style. In-text citations are present and accurate. The student can produce a bibliography without consulting the style guide for common entry types.

**Developing slowly:** Bibliographies are missing entries, incorrectly formatted, or inconsistent. The student treats citation as a chore rather than a practice.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student worked through enough examples? Citation format is learned through repetition, not explanation. Have you provided feedback on early bibliographies and required corrections?

### 4. Plagiarism Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can identify plagiarism in sample passages, explain why it matters, and articulate the difference between proper paraphrase and patchwriting. They cite as a matter of habit, not as a response to requirement.

**Developing slowly:** The student treats plagiarism as "something you get in trouble for" without understanding the underlying principle. May not see paraphrase without citation as problematic.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you discussed plagiarism as a character issue rather than a rule issue? Has the student seen real examples and worked through gray areas?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Teaching Citation Format Without Teaching Why
Students who learn MLA or Chicago format as arbitrary rules to memorize will resent the practice and forget the format. Students who understand that every citation is an invitation to the reader to verify the claim — and that this verification is what makes shared knowledge possible — will internalize the practice. Teach the why before the how.

### Assigning Research Without Modeling It
A student who has never seen an adult conduct research does not know what the process looks like. Show them. Research alongside them. Let them see you evaluate a source, take notes, discover that your first question was not quite right and revise it. The process is as important as the product.

### Accepting Sloppy Notes
If you do not check notes until the paper is due, the student has been practicing bad habits for weeks. Check notes early — after the first research session. Correct problems immediately. Good note-taking is a habit, and habits are formed (or deformed) through early practice.

### Making Plagiarism Scary Instead of Serious
Fear-based approaches to plagiarism produce students who are anxious about citation rather than committed to honesty. The goal is a student who cites because they believe in intellectual integrity, not a student who over-cites because they are terrified of getting caught. Keep the tone serious but not punitive.
